description_en: The mainstream body of Muslims who follow the Quran, the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ, and the understanding of the Companions. Identified by the Prophet ﷺ as the saved sect (al-firqah an-najiyah) in the hadith of the 73 sects. Encompasses the four schools of fiqh (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) and the accepted aqeedah schools (Athari, Ash'ari, Maturidi).
key_beliefs: Following Quran and Sunnah upon the understanding of the Companions (Salaf)
Accepting the legitimacy of all four Rightly Guided Caliphs
Four major schools of jurisprudence (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali)
Affirming Allah's attributes as revealed without distortion or denial
Belief in predestination (qadr) including its good and evil
scholarly_evaluation: The saved sect (al-firqah an-najiyah) mentioned in the hadith of the 73 sects (Sunan al-Tirmidhi, 2641). Represents the majority of Muslims throughout Islamic history. Defined not merely by label but by adherence to the Quran, Sunnah, and methodology of the Companions.
key_figures: The Four Rightly Guided Caliphs (Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali)

description_en: The creed of accepting the apparent meaning of Quran and Sunnah regarding divine attributes without ta'wil (metaphorical interpretation), takyeef (asking how), or tamtheel (comparison to creation). The methodology of the Salaf al-Salih (pious predecessors). Also called the creed of the Ahl al-Hadith. Championed by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal and later by Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim.
key_beliefs: Affirming all of Allah's attributes as they appear in text without interpretation
Tafwid al-Kayfiyyah — entrusting the 'how' to Allah without asking 'how'
Strict adherence to the methodology of the Companions and Tabi'un
Rejecting kalam (speculative theology) as a methodology for aqeedah
scholarly_evaluation: The primary aqeedah position of Ahl us-Sunnah. The methodology of the early Muslim community (Salaf). Championed by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal in his refutation of the Mu'tazilah during the Mihna (833 CE). Later systematized by Ibn Taymiyyah (d. 728 AH) in his works al-Aqeedah al-Wasitiyyah, al-Aqeedah al-Hamawiyyah, and Minhaj al-Sunnah.

description_en: A school of Islamic theology founded by Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari (d. 935 CE) that uses limited rational argumentation to defend the creed of Ahl us-Sunnah against Mu'tazilah rationalism. Accepts Allah's attributes but differs from the Athari position in its use of ta'wil (metaphorical interpretation) for some attributes. Predominant among Shafi'i and Maliki scholars historically.
key_beliefs: Allah's existence and attributes proven through rational argument
Limited ta'wil of ambiguous Quranic verses regarding divine attributes
Seven essential attributes: Life, Knowledge, Power, Will, Hearing, Sight, Speech
Rejecting the Mu'tazilah position that the Quran is created
scholarly_evaluation: Accepted within Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah by majority scholarly consensus, despite methodological differences with the Athari school on certain matters. Al-Ash'ari himself was a student of al-Jubbai (Mu'tazilah) before his return to the creed of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, which he documented in Al-Ibanah. Historically represented by major scholars including al-Bayhaqi, al-Nawawi, Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, and al-Suyuti.
sources: Al-Luma' fi al-Radd ala Ahl al-Zaigh (al-Ash'ari)

description_en: A school of Islamic theology founded by Abu Mansur al-Maturidi (d. 944 CE) in Samarkand. Similar to the Ash'ari school in defending Ahl us-Sunnah creed through rational argument, with some distinct positions — most notably on the attributes of Allah and on human agency. Predominant in Central Asia and historically the dominant aqeedah school among Hanafi scholars.
key_beliefs: Allah's existence and attributes provable through reason
Affirming all of Allah's essential attributes
Human beings have kasb (acquisition) of their actions while Allah is the creator of all acts
Faith (iman) is affirming with the heart — verbal statement is a condition, not part of iman itself
scholarly_evaluation: Accepted within Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah alongside the Ash'ari school. Historically the dominant aqeedah school among Hanafi scholars in Central Asia, the Indian subcontinent, and the Ottoman world. Minor differences from Ash'ari and Athari on specific theological points do not place it outside Ahl us-Sunnah.

founder_name: Imam Abu Hanifa al-Nu'man ibn Thabit
description_en: One of the four major Sunni schools of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh), founded by Imam Abu Hanifa (699–767 CE) in Kufa, Iraq. Known for its extensive use of qiyas (analogical reasoning) and istihsan (juristic preference). Predominant in Turkey, Central Asia, South Asia, the Balkans, and historically in the Ottoman Caliphate.
key_beliefs: Following Quran, Sunnah, Ijma (scholarly consensus), and Qiyas (analogical reasoning)
Broad use of Istihsan (juristic preference) where strict analogy produces hardship
Consideration of local custom ('urf) in legal rulings
Known for flexibility in removing undue hardship (rafah) from religious obligations
scholarly_evaluation: One of the four accepted madhabs of Ahl us-Sunnah. Fully within the saved sect. Founded by Imam Abu Hanifa, one of the greatest jurists in Islamic history, known as 'al-Imam al-A'zam' (the Great Imam). Following any of the four madhabs is valid and praiseworthy.

description_en: One of the four major Sunni schools of jurisprudence founded by Imam Malik ibn Anas (711–795 CE) in Medina. Distinctive for its use of the practice of the people of Medina (amal ahl al-Madinah) as a legal source, given that Medina was the city of the Prophet ﷺ and the Companions. Predominant in North Africa, West Africa, Andalusia (historically), and parts of the Gulf.
key_beliefs: Following Quran, Sunnah, Ijma, and the practice of the people of Medina
The 'amal (practice) of the Madinans is a primary legal source
Maslahah mursalah (public interest) used in legislation
Strong emphasis on Hadith of the people of Madinah
scholarly_evaluation: One of the four accepted madhabs of Ahl us-Sunnah. Imam Malik's al-Muwatta is considered one of the earliest and most authoritative hadith collections. Imam al-Shafi'i famously studied under Imam Malik and described him as a proof of Allah on His creation.

description_en: One of the four major Sunni schools of jurisprudence founded by Imam al-Shafi'i (767–820 CE). Imam al-Shafi'i is widely regarded as the founder of Islamic legal theory (usul al-fiqh), having systematized the sources of Islamic law in his landmark work al-Risalah. Predominant in East Africa, Southeast Asia, Egypt, the Levant, and parts of the Arabian Peninsula.
key_beliefs: Following Quran, Sunnah, Ijma, and Qiyas — the four sources of al-Shafi'i's legal theory
The Sunnah must be authenticated before use as a legal source
Strict criteria for the use of Qiyas compared to the Hanafi school
Systematized distinction between Makki and Madani jurisprudence
scholarly_evaluation: One of the four accepted madhabs of Ahl us-Sunnah. Imam al-Shafi'i's al-Risalah (c. 820 CE) is the foundational text of Islamic legal theory. He studied under Imam Malik and was himself a teacher of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

description_en: One of the four major Sunni schools of jurisprudence, founded by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal (780–855 CE). Known for strict adherence to Hadith and the narrations of the Companions in legal matters, using qiyas only when necessary. Imam Ahmad's famous stand against the Mu'tazilah in the Mihna crisis (833 CE) cemented his status as a defender of Ahl us-Sunnah. Predominant in Saudi Arabia and parts of the Gulf.
key_beliefs: Strict prioritization of Hadith and athar (narrations of Companions) in legal derivation
Preference for weak hadith over pure qiyas in the absence of stronger evidence
Strong emphasis on the creed of the Salaf and rejection of speculative kalam
Imam Ahmad's creed (Usul al-Sunnah) is the reference for Athari aqeedah
scholarly_evaluation: One of the four accepted madhabs of Ahl us-Sunnah. Imam Ahmad is considered one of the foremost defenders of Sunni Islam. His refusal to affirm the Mu'tazili doctrine that the Quran was created — even under torture during the Mihna — made him a symbol of steadfastness for Ahl us-Sunnah throughout history.

description_en: A historical school of Islamic jurisprudence founded by Dawud ibn Khalaf al-Zahiri (815–883 CE), later championed by Ibn Hazm of Andalusia (994–1064 CE). Takes only the literal (zahir) meaning of the Quran and Sunnah, rejecting qiyas (analogical reasoning) and ra'y (personal opinion) as legal sources. No longer has institutionalized following today but its methodology continues to influence Islamic scholarship.
key_beliefs: Strict literalism — only the apparent (zahir) meaning of Quran and Sunnah is binding
Complete rejection of qiyas (analogical reasoning) as a legal source
Ijma (consensus) limited to the consensus of the Companions only
Every act is permissible unless explicitly prohibited by a text
scholarly_evaluation: A legitimate historical school within Ahl us-Sunnah. While not followed today as an organized madhab, Ibn Hazm's encyclopedic works (especially Al-Muhalla) are standard references for comparative fiqh. His rejection of qiyas was a minority position but not outside the pale of Ahl us-Sunnah.

description_en: A historical school of Islamic jurisprudence founded by the great scholar and historian Ibn Jarir al-Tabari (839–923 CE). Ibn al-Tabari was an independent mujtahid who founded his own school, though it did not survive long after his death due to lack of students to carry it forward. He is primarily remembered today for his monumental Tafsir (Jami al-Bayan) and his world history (Tarikh al-Rusul wal-Muluk).
key_beliefs: Independent ijtihad from Quran and Sunnah with no strict affiliation to earlier schools
Strong emphasis on hadith and athar in legal derivation
Acceptance of some forms of qiyas where necessary
scholarly_evaluation: A legitimate historical school within Ahl us-Sunnah. The school did not outlive its founder institutionally, but Ibn al-Tabari's jurisprudential and tafsir works remain authoritative references.
sources: Jami al-Bayan fi Ta'wil Ay al-Quran (al-Tabari)

description_en: The first major deviant sect in Islamic history, emerging in 657 CE (37 AH) after the Battle of Siffin. They rejected the arbitration between Ali ibn Abi Talib and Muawiyah, declaring that judgment belongs to Allah alone (la hukma illa lillah). They held that Muslims who commit major sins become kafir and are to be killed, and they rebelled against the Muslim rulers of their time. The Prophet ﷺ described them as 'dogs of the Fire' who would recite the Quran without it passing their throats.
key_beliefs: Takfir of Muslims who commit major sins — declaring them disbelievers
Armed rebellion against Muslim rulers is obligatory when they sin
Rejection of the legitimacy of both Ali and Muawiyah after the arbitration
Extreme literalism without understanding the broader principles of the Sunnah
scholarly_evaluation: Universally condemned by Ahl us-Sunnah as a deviant sect. The Prophet ﷺ described them in multiple authentic hadiths as 'dogs of the Fire' (Sunan Ibn Majah, 173) and 'the worst of creation' (Sahih Muslim, 1066). Their most distinctive characteristic — takfir of Muslims and killing them — is identified as their primary error by Ahl us-Sunnah. Modern groups that apply this methodology (declaring Muslim rulers or ordinary Muslims kafir for their actions and taking up arms) are considered neo-Khawarij.
key_figures: Abdullah ibn Wahhab al-Rasibi (first Kharijite leader)
Dhul-Khuwaysira al-Tamimi (earliest Kharijite tendency, rebuked by the Prophet ﷺ)

description_en: An early deviant sect that denied Allah's predestination (qadar), holding that human beings create their own actions independently of Allah's will and foreknowledge. Emerged in Basra around 680 CE. Ibn Umar reportedly called them 'Majus of this Ummah' because, like the Zoroastrians who posited two creators (good and evil), the Qadariyyah in effect posited that humans create their own actions independently of Allah.
key_beliefs: Denial of Allah's predestination of human actions
Human beings create their own actions independently
Allah has no foreknowledge of human sins (extreme position)
scholarly_evaluation: Condemned by the Companions and the Tabi'un. Abdullah ibn Umar reportedly refused to pray over them or greet them with salam when they abandoned belief in qadar (Sahih Muslim, introduction). The Prophet ﷺ said: 'The Qadariyyah are the Majus of this Ummah' (Sunan Abu Dawud, 4691). Their view contradicts the explicit Quranic affirmation of qadar (al-Qamar 54:49, al-Hadid 57:22). The Mu'tazilah later inherited and systematized elements of this position.
key_figures: Ma'bad al-Juhani (d. 699 CE) — early Qadariyyah leader, killed in battle

description_en: An early theological school that emerged in the late 7th century as a reaction to the Khawarij extremism on faith and sin. They held that actions are not part of faith (iman), and that as long as one holds belief in the heart, sins do not harm one's faith or status as a Muslim. The name derives from irja' (postponement) — they 'postponed' judgment on Muslim sinners to Allah alone.
key_beliefs: Faith (iman) consists only of heart belief — actions are not part of faith
No Muslim who holds correct belief in the heart can be harmed by sin
Judgment on sinners is deferred entirely to Allah — no distinction between levels of faith
scholarly_evaluation: Condemned by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal and Ahl us-Sunnah scholars as a deviant position on faith. While they erred on the relationship between faith and actions, they did not typically reach the level of outside-the-fold as they did not deny any fundamentals of the deen. Ahl us-Sunnah holds that faith increases and decreases, and that actions are part of faith. Note: Some Murji'ah positions influenced later Hanafi discussions on the definition of iman — but classical Hanafis are not Murji'ah; they have distinct nuances.
key_figures: Hasan ibn Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah (attributed early sympathy)
sources: Majmu' al-Fatawa (Ibn Taymiyyah) — on iman and its components

description_en: A deviant theological sect founded by Jahm ibn Safwan (d. 745 CE), who was executed for his heterodox positions. Held that Allah has no attributes whatsoever (ta'til — complete negation of attributes), that the Quran is created, and that faith is only knowledge in the heart without affirmation or action. Jahm was influenced by Indian and Greek philosophical ideas. Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal wrote extensively against the Jahmiyyah.
key_beliefs: Complete negation of all divine attributes (ta'til)
The Quran is created — Allah's speech is created (the position that triggered the Mihna)
Faith is knowledge in the heart alone — verbal affirmation not required
Paradise and Hell are not eternal — they will eventually cease
scholarly_evaluation: Harshly condemned by Ahl us-Sunnah. Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal's primary battle during the Mihna (833 CE) was against the Mu'tazilah and Jahmiyyah doctrine that the Quran was created. Ibn Taymiyyah wrote al-Radd 'ala al-Jahmiyyah (Refutation of the Jahmiyyah). Jahm ibn Safwan was killed by Salm ibn Ahwaz al-Mazini for his deviance. The Ash'ari and Maturidi schools both developed partly in response to Jahmiyyah excess.
sources: Al-Radd ala al-Jahmiyyah wal-Zanadiqa (Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal)

description_en: A rationalist theological school that emerged in Basra around 750 CE under Wasil ibn Ata after he parted from the circle of al-Hasan al-Basri. Prioritized rational philosophy (kalam) over transmitted text (naql) in matters of theology. Most notably held that the Quran was created, denied seven of Allah's attributes (claiming affirming them creates partners), and held that human beings create their own actions (free will over divine predestination). The 'Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun imposed their doctrine as state creed in the Mihna (833 CE), under which Imam Ahmad was tortured.
key_beliefs: The Quran is created (not the eternal speech of Allah)
Denial of most divine attributes to avoid perceived tashbih (likening to creation)
Human beings create their own actions — divine justice requires human free will
The rational mind can independently determine good and evil (husn wa qubh 'aqli)
Sinners in the 'intermediate position' — neither believer nor disbeliever
scholarly_evaluation: Condemned by Ahl us-Sunnah. Their political power ended after Caliph al-Mutawakkil abolished the Mihna in 848 CE and restored Sunni creed as state doctrine. The Ash'ari and Maturidi schools developed in part as a rational counter-argument to Mu'tazilah using their own philosophical tools. Despite this, some Mu'tazilah scholars contributed greatly to Arabic grammar, literary criticism, and Islamic jurisprudence — their theological positions, however, are rejected.

description_en: A moderate offshoot from the early Khawarij movement, founded by Abdullah ibn Ibad in 684 CE. Unlike the Khawarij, the Ibadis do not consider ordinary Muslim sinners to be kafir — they consider them 'ungrateful disbelievers' (kuffar ni'mah) rather than polytheists. They reject armed rebellion and accept peaceful coexistence. Concentrated today primarily in Oman, Zanzibar, and parts of Libya and Algeria. The Ibadi community in Oman is the world's most significant.
key_beliefs: Derived from Khawarij but without the extreme takfir of ordinary Muslim sinners
Leaders (Imams) must be elected on merit, not dynastic succession
The Quran is created (adopting Mu'tazilah-adjacent position on this point)
Walayah (loyalty) to those who follow their positions; bara'ah (disassociation) from those who oppose
scholarly_evaluation: Classified as deviant by Ahl us-Sunnah due to its Kharijite origins and retained heterodox positions. However, the Ibadis' moderation relative to classical Khawarij — rejecting takfir and armed rebellion — means most Ahl us-Sunnah scholars do not consider them fully outside the fold. Their position on the Quran being created, however, is a serious error shared with the Mu'tazilah.
key_figures: Abdullah ibn Ibad al-Murri al-Tamimi (d. circa 708 CE)

description_en: A modern movement rejecting the authority of the Hadith and Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ, claiming the Quran alone is sufficient. Known by various names including Ahl al-Quran, Quraniyoon, and Submitters. Despite modern framing, this is a revival of the classical heresy of rejecting the Sunnah, which was addressed by Imam al-Shafi'i in his al-Risalah (c. 820 CE). Emerged in South Asian reformist movements in the late 19th century and has spread through the internet in the 20th–21st centuries.
key_beliefs: Rejection of the Hadith and Sunnah as a source of Islamic law
The Quran alone is the complete and sufficient guide
Salah, Zakat, Hajj methods not derived from Hadith but from 'Quranic principles'
Many Quranic commands (like the details of prayer) reinterpreted entirely
scholarly_evaluation: Condemned unanimously by Ahl us-Sunnah. Allah commands obedience to the Messenger ﷺ alongside obedience to Himself in more than 30 Quranic verses (e.g., al-Nisa 4:59, al-Hashr 59:7). The Hadith are the primary explanation of the Quran. Rejection of the Sunnah destroys the foundations of Islamic practice — prayer timings, rak'ahs, zakat thresholds, and hajj rites are not detailed in the Quran but in the Sunnah. Imam al-Shafi'i's al-Risalah explicitly refutes this position in the 9th century.
key_figures: Ghulam Ahmad Pervez (1903–1985 CE) — prominent South Asian Quranist
Rashad Khalifa (1935–1990 CE) — founded the 'Submitters' movement
sources: Al-Risalah (al-Shafi'i) — refutation of rejecting the Sunnah
Al-Quran 4:59, 59:7 — commands to obey the Messenger

description_en: Shia Islam is a broad umbrella encompassing various groups that arose following the assassination of Ali ibn Abi Talib (661 CE / 40 AH) and hold that the Imamate should have passed through the lineage of Ali and Fatimah. Theological and political positions range widely — from the relatively moderate Zaydis to the extreme Rafidah. Ahl us-Sunnah scholarship distinguishes carefully between ordinary Shia laymen (potentially within the fold) and groups with specific doctrinal positions that place them outside it.
key_beliefs: The Imamate belongs exclusively to the lineage of Ali ibn Abi Talib
The legitimacy of Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman's caliphates is rejected
The Imam has special religious authority over the community
scholarly_evaluation: Ahl us-Sunnah scholars have nuanced positions on Shia Muslims. The broad Shia category includes groups ranging from deviant (Zaydi, mainstream Twelver) to those outside the fold entirely (Rafidah, Ismaili). The key distinctions are: (1) Does the person curse the Companions? (2) Do they invoke dead Imams in du'a? (3) Do they hold the Quran was corrupted? Ordinary Shia laymen who simply believe Ali had a stronger right to caliphate but do not curse companions are not declared kafir by most Ahl us-Sunnah scholars.
key_figures: Ali ibn Abi Talib (4th Caliph, 1st Shia Imam in Shia framework)
Al-Husayn ibn Ali (3rd Imam, d. 680 CE/61 AH at Karbala)

description_en: The Zaydi branch of Shia Islam, named after Zayd ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn, who revolted against the Umayyad Caliph Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik in 740 CE. Of all the Shia groups, the Zaydis are closest to Ahl us-Sunnah in theology and jurisprudence. They do not believe in the infallibility (ismah) of Imams, do not invoke dead Imams, and accept the first three caliphs as legitimate (if not the most excellent). They believe Ali was more deserving but do not curse Abu Bakr or Umar. Concentrated today primarily in Yemen.
key_beliefs: Ali was the most deserving of the caliphate but Abu Bakr and Umar's rule is considered valid
Imams are not infallible — they can sin and err
The Imam must be from the Prophet's family (Ahl al-Bayt) and must rise in revolt
Jurisprudence close to Hanafi and Mu'tazilah in some rulings
scholarly_evaluation: The closest Shia group to Ahl us-Sunnah. Many Ahl us-Sunnah scholars have noted that Zaydis who do not curse the Companions and do not invoke dead Imams may be within the fold of Islam despite their deviation on the Imamate question. Ibn Taymiyyah and others acknowledged this distinction. Their proximity to Ahl us-Sunnah is why some classical scholars treated them differently from the Rafidah.

description_en: The largest Shia group, following twelve Imams from Ali ibn Abi Talib to Muhammad al-Mahdi, the twelfth Imam who is believed to have entered occultation in 874 CE (260 AH) and will return at the End of Times. Predominant in Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Bahrain, and parts of Pakistan. The mainstream Twelver doctrine — as found in the primary religious texts (al-Kafi) — includes beliefs that Ahl us-Sunnah scholars consider shirk, particularly invoking the Imams in du'a after their deaths.
key_beliefs: Twelve Infallible Imams from the lineage of Ali and Fatimah
The twelfth Imam (al-Mahdi) is in occultation and will return
The Imams have knowledge of the unseen (a divine-like attribute)
Wilayah (devotion) to the Imams is a pillar of faith
Intercession (tawassul) through the Imams after their deaths
scholarly_evaluation: The mainstream Twelver doctrinal positions — particularly invoking dead Imams in du'a and attributing knowledge of the unseen to them — are considered shirk by Athari and majority Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. This places the doctrinal Twelver position in serious territory. However, a distinction is drawn between (1) ordinary Shia laymen who follow family tradition, love Ahl al-Bayt, and do not knowingly invoke dead Imams in the shirk sense, and (2) those who knowingly hold the full doctrinal positions. Individual takfir of ordinary Shia laymen is not the Ahl us-Sunnah position — the doctrinal group positions are evaluated, not individual hearts. The Rafidah — those who explicitly curse companions and claim Quranic corruption — are a separate, more extreme category (see Rafidah entry).
key_figures: Ja'far al-Sadiq (6th Imam, d. 765 CE) — major source of Shia jurisprudence
Muhammad al-Mahdi (12th Imam, entered occultation 874 CE)

description_en: The term applied to extreme Shia who explicitly curse and revile the Companions of the Prophet ﷺ — especially Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman — and hold that the Quran was corrupted by the Companions after the Prophet's death. Distinguished from ordinary Shia by three defining positions: cursing the Sahabah, claiming Quranic corruption, and ascribing divine attributes (infallibility, knowledge of the unseen) to the Imams — including invoking them in du'a after death.
key_beliefs: Explicit cursing of Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and most Companions
Claim that the Companions corrupted the Quran after the Prophet's death
Infallibility and divine knowledge of the twelve Imams
Invocation of dead Imams in du'a (shirk per Athari aqeedah)
Wilayah (guardianship/authority) of Imams as a pillar of faith equal to the shahada
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam according to Ibn Taymiyyah (Minhaj al-Sunnah), Ibn Kathir, al-Dhahabi, and the majority of Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. Three doctrines are specifically cited: (1) The claim that the Companions corrupted the Quran — contradicting Allah's own guarantee in the Quran: 'We have sent down the Dhikr and We will preserve it' (15:9). (2) Explicit cursing of Abu Bakr and Umar, who were praised by the Prophet ﷺ. (3) Invocation of dead Imams — shirk according to Athari aqeedah. Note: Ordinary Shia who believe only in Ali's right to caliphate without these extreme positions are considered deviant Muslims within the fold, not kafirs. The label Rafidah applies specifically to those who hold these three positions.
key_figures: Abdullah ibn Saba (attributed instigator of early Shia fitna)
Al-Kulayni (d. 941 CE — compiler of al-Kafi, the primary Twelver hadith collection)

description_en: A Shia branch that diverged from the mainstream Twelvers by following Ismail ibn Ja'far as the seventh Imam rather than his brother Musa al-Kadhim. Developed highly esoteric (batini) doctrines, holding that all Quranic texts have a hidden inner meaning (batin) that supersedes the literal (zahir) meaning — in practice removing the obligation of the shari'ah from their followers. Includes sub-groups: Nizari Ismaili (largest, the Aga Khan's followers), Bohra, and the Fatimid dynasty historically.
key_beliefs: Esoteric (batini) interpretation of Quran and Shari'ah — outer obligations are superseded by inner meanings
The Imam (currently the Aga Khan IV) has divine authority and near-infallible guidance
Many Sharia obligations (prayer, fasting, hajj) are allegorized away for the initiated
Neo-Platonic and Gnostic philosophical framework integrated into their theology
scholarly_evaluation: Declared outside the fold of Islam by Ibn Taymiyyah (Minhaj al-Sunnah), al-Ghazali (Fada'ih al-Batiniyyah — specifically a refutation of the Ismaili Batiniyyah), and majority Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. Al-Ghazali's Fada'ih al-Batiniyyah is a landmark refutation. Their abrogation of the obligatory acts of Islam through esoteric interpretation is a categorical departure from the religion.
key_figures: Al-Hasan ibn al-Sabbah (1050–1124 CE) — founded Nizari Assassin sect
Aga Khan IV (b. 1936) — current Nizari Imam

description_en: Organized spiritual brotherhoods (tariqahs) that emerged in the Islamic world from around the 9th–10th centuries CE. Each order is centered on a founding saint (Shaykh) whose spiritual chain (silsilah) is traced back to the Prophet ﷺ, and involves bay'ah (pledge of allegiance) to a living shaykh, distinctive dhikr formulas, initiation rites, and hierarchical spiritual progression. The institution of organized tariqahs — as distinct from Sufi spirituality in general — is considered bid'ah (innovation) by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. Some orders have developed practices that cross into shirk, particularly invoking dead saints (istighatha).
key_beliefs: Bay'ah (pledge) to a shaykh whose spiritual guidance is considered essential to the path
Silsilah (spiritual chain) as a transmission of baraka from the Prophet ﷺ through the shaykh
Specialized dhikr formulas, often kept secret within the order
Spiritual stations (maqamat) and states (ahwal) as the path to proximity to Allah
Intercession through saints — in many orders, du'a directed to deceased founders
scholarly_evaluation: The institution of organized tariqahs is not established by the Quran, Sunnah, or the practice of the Salaf — making it bid'ah. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Every newly invented thing is an innovation, and every innovation is going astray' (Sahih Muslim 867). The bay'ah given to a shaykh for spiritual purposes has no basis in the Sunnah. Individual practices within orders vary widely — some tariqahs remain within the bounds of the Sunnah in their actual practice, while others have developed shirk-adjacent rites. The key line for individual Muslims is: invoking dead saints (istighatha) is shirk regardless of the tariqah. Ordinary Muslims within tariqahs who avoid these specific practices are not declared kafir.
key_figures: Al-Junayd al-Baghdadi (d. 910 CE) — considered a legitimate early Sufi scholar
Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani (1077–1166 CE) — founder of the Qadiriyya
Ibn Arabi (1165–1240 CE) — controversial; his doctrine of wahdat al-wujud (unity of being) is considered kufr by many scholars
sources: Sahih Muslim 867 (every innovation is going astray)

description_en: The oldest and one of the most widespread Sufi orders, named after Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani (1077–1166 CE) of Baghdad. Has tens of millions of followers across Africa, South Asia, and the Middle East. The order emphasizes the practice of dhikr and service to creation as spiritual disciplines. Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani himself is venerated to a degree that has crossed into grave worship in many branches of the order.
key_beliefs: Silsilah traced to Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani then to the Prophet ﷺ via Ali ibn Abi Talib
Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani considered a Qutb (Pole) of the universe, with cosmic spiritual authority
Dhikr sessions as the primary spiritual practice
Visitation of Abd al-Qadir's tomb and seeking his intercession (widespread in popular practice)
scholarly_evaluation: The order's bid'ah institution aside, the primary theological concern is the popular veneration of Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani that has evolved in many branches into grave worship and invocation of the dead — both of which are shirk per Ahl us-Sunnah. Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani himself was a Hanbali scholar whose authenticated writings are free of these excesses — they were added by later followers.
sources: Futuh al-Ghayb (Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani — authentic works)

description_en: A major Sufi order founded by Baha-ud-Din Naqshband Bukhari (1318–1389 CE) in Central Asia. One of the most influential orders globally, with major presence in Central Asia, Turkey, the Indian subcontinent, and the Caucasus. Known for silent dhikr (khafi) rather than audible group dhikr. Has historically been involved in political resistance movements.
key_beliefs: Silsilah claimed through Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (unique among orders)
Silent (khafi) dhikr as the primary spiritual practice
Muraqabah (meditation) as a spiritual station
Strong emphasis on adherence to the Sunnah outwardly
scholarly_evaluation: The bid'ah of the organized tariqah institution applies here as with other orders. The Naqshbandiyya's outward emphasis on Sunnah adherence is noted — some sub-branches are closer to orthodox practice than others. The claim of silsilah through Abu Bakr al-Siddiq is contested historically.
key_figures: Ahmad Sirhindi (1564–1624 CE) — major reforming figure

description_en: A Sufi order founded in Ajmer, India by Moinuddin Chishti (d. 1236 CE). One of the most popular orders in South Asia, known for the use of qawwali (devotional music) as a spiritual practice, and the veneration of shrines (dargahs) of Sufi saints. The order holds that music and sama' (listening to spiritual music) are legitimate paths to spiritual states.
key_beliefs: Sama' (listening to spiritual music including qawwali) as a path to spiritual states
Veneration of the shrines of Sufi saints (dargahs)
Universal love as the core spiritual value
Seeking intercession from the saints buried in dargahs
scholarly_evaluation: The use of music as a spiritual practice and the veneration of saint shrines are both condemned by Ahl us-Sunnah. The Prophet ﷺ forbade musical instruments in numerous hadiths (Sahih al-Bukhari 5590) and forbade building structures over graves (Sahih Muslim 969). Dargah visitation practices — including seeking intercession from the dead — constitute shirk per Athari aqeedah.

description_en: A Sufi order founded in North Africa by Ahmad al-Tijani (1735–1815 CE). Highly influential in West Africa (Senegal, Mali, Nigeria, Mauritania). Claims that the founder received the Salat al-Fatih directly from the Prophet ﷺ in a waking vision, that this prayer is equivalent to 600,000 recitations of the entire Quran, and that members of the order are guaranteed paradise regardless of their deeds. The order also forbids its members from participating in other Sufi orders.
key_beliefs: Ahmad al-Tijani received the Salat al-Fatih directly from the Prophet ﷺ in a waking vision
The Salat al-Fatih is equivalent to 600,000 recitations of the entire Quran
Members of the order are guaranteed paradise
Exclusive membership — joining another tariqah is forbidden
Ahmad al-Tijani claimed the title of Khatm al-Awliya (Seal of the Awliya)
scholarly_evaluation: Among the more severely condemned Sufi orders by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. The claim of receiving revelation or hadith directly from the Prophet ﷺ in a waking vision after the Prophet's death contradicts the closure of prophethood and is itself a deviant innovation. The guarantee of paradise for order members regardless of deeds contradicts numerous Quranic verses and hadiths. Extensively criticized by Mauritanian and Senegalese Salafi scholars.
sources: Jawahir al-Ma'ani (Ali Harazim — primary Tijaniyya text)

description_en: A Sufi order founded by Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili (1197–1258 CE) in North Africa. Prominent in Egypt, the Maghreb, and parts of the Levant. Known for its relative emphasis on engaging in the world rather than complete withdrawal, and for famous litanies (awrad) such as the Hizb al-Bahr attributed to al-Shadhili.
key_beliefs: Spiritual path through inner purification (tazkiyah) combined with worldly engagement
Awrad (litanies) transmitted through the silsilah as a spiritual practice
Al-Shadhili taught that the external shari'ah must be maintained alongside inner spiritual work
Hierarchical spiritual stations (maqamat) leading to proximity to Allah
scholarly_evaluation: The Shadhiliyya is considered one of the more moderate Sufi orders historically, with al-Shadhili himself emphasizing shari'ah adherence. The bid'ah of the organized tariqah institution still applies. Individual practices within branches vary — some have developed grave veneration practices condemned by Ahl us-Sunnah.
key_figures: Ibn Ata Allah al-Iskandari (d. 1309 CE) — author of al-Hikam al-Ata'iyyah

description_en: A Sufi order founded in Konya (present-day Turkey) based on the teachings of Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207–1273 CE). Famous for the Sema ceremony — a ritualistic whirling dance performed as a form of dhikr. Rumi's Persian poetry (the Masnavi) is beloved across the Muslim world and beyond, though his works contain philosophical positions that scholars have contested, including elements influenced by Neo-Platonic and pantheistic thought.
key_beliefs: Sema (whirling dance) as a form of worship and spiritual practice
Universal love as the path to Allah — 'Come, come, whoever you are'
Rumi's Masnavi as a spiritual guide
The soul's longing for its divine origin as the core spiritual theme
scholarly_evaluation: The Sema ceremony (ritual whirling as worship) has no basis in the Sunnah and is bid'ah. Rumi's Masnavi contains both genuine spiritual insight and philosophically problematic material — including statements that have been interpreted as wahdat al-wujud (pantheism/union with God), which is considered kufr by many Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. The order is prominent in Turkey today as a cultural institution and UNESCO intangible heritage, which has further secularized it.
key_figures: Sultan Walad (Rumi's son, organized the order)

description_en: A reformist movement within Ahl us-Sunnah calling for a return to the methodology of the Salaf al-Salih (pious predecessors) in all matters of creed, worship, and practice. The Salafi methodology is not a new sect but a call to return to the Athari approach of the early Muslims. Major scholars associated with this methodology include Ibn Taymiyyah (1263–1328 CE), Ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703–1792 CE), and 20th-century scholars such as Ibn Baz, al-Albani, and Ibn Uthaymin.
key_beliefs: Return to the Quran and Sunnah upon the methodology of the Companions
Rejection of taqlid (blind following) in favor of following evidence
Strict Athari aqeedah — affirming Allah's attributes without ta'wil
Rejection of bid'ah in worship practices
Varying positions on political engagement — generally quietist (Madkhali) to activist (Muslim Brotherhood-adjacent)
scholarly_evaluation: The Salafi call — at its core — is identical to the call of Ahl us-Sunnah throughout history. It is a reform movement, not a new sect. However, the label 'Salafi' has been claimed by groups with varying methodologies, including some who have adopted Kharijite-adjacent political positions (takfiri Salafism, Salafi-jihadism) — which are entirely condemned by mainstream Salafi scholars and are not part of the Salafi methodology.

description_en: A reformist movement that emerged in 19th-century South Asia, closely paralleling the Salafi movement and sharing its core methodology: return to Quran and Sunnah, rejection of strict madhab taqlid, and Athari aqeedah. Distinguished from the Deobandi and Barelvi movements by its emphasis on following authenticated hadith over traditional madhab positions and its rejection of much of popular South Asian religious practice.
key_beliefs: Following the Quran and Sunnah directly without rigid madhab taqlid
Athari aqeedah — close alignment with the Hanbali and Salafi positions
Rejection of Sufi practices, shrine visitation, and mawlid celebrations
Use of authenticated hadith as the primary criterion in legal disputes
scholarly_evaluation: Within Ahl us-Sunnah. The Ahl-e-Hadith movement's core call is the same as the Salafi and Hanbali positions on aqeedah and hadith-adherence. The label does not refer to early hadith scholars (Imam Ahmad, al-Bukhari, etc.) but to this specific South Asian reformist movement. Its positions on aqeedah and rejection of bid'ah are in line with mainstream Ahl us-Sunnah.

founder_name: Muhammad Qasim Nanautawi and Rashid Ahmad Gangohi
description_en: A major Islamic reform movement founded in Deoband, India in 1867 CE, named after the Darul Uloom Deoband seminary. Emerged as a response to British colonial rule and the decline of Muslim political power. Follows the Hanafi madhab in fiqh and is primarily Maturidi in aqeedah, but has strong Deobandi positions on various issues including the prohibition of mawlid celebrations and specific practices it considers bid'ah. Has significant presence in South Asia, the UK, and South Africa.
key_beliefs: Strict Hanafi jurisprudence with rejection of practices considered bid'ah
Maturidi aqeedah with some Athari-adjacent positions
Strong emphasis on hadith studies through traditional institutions
Rejection of many popular South Asian religious practices
scholarly_evaluation: The Deobandi movement is positioned on the border of Ahl us-Sunnah. Its core call to follow the Sunnah and reject clear innovations is sound. However, scholarly concerns relate to: (1) Specific statements in early Deobandi texts (Barahin-e-Qati'ah, Hifzul Iman) that appear to make impermissible comparisons between the Prophet's ﷺ knowledge and others — condemned by many Ahl us-Sunnah scholars including Ahmad Rida Khan. (2) Affiliation with Sufi tariqahs (Chishti, Naqshbandi, etc.) — the same bid'ah applies. (3) Some positions on the permissibility of certain practices remain contested. The movement overall is closer to Ahl us-Sunnah than the Barelvi school but is not free of issues.

description_en: A major Islamic movement founded by Ahmad Rida Khan (1856–1921 CE) in Bareilly, India. Characterized by strong veneration of the Prophet ﷺ and Islamic saints, Mawlid celebrations, shrine visitation (urs), and extensive Sufi practices. Dominant in the rural areas of Pakistan, Bangladesh, and parts of the UK. Strongly opposed to the Deobandi and Ahl-e-Hadith movements, issuing takfir against early Deobandi scholars.
key_beliefs: The Prophet ﷺ has knowledge of the unseen (ilm al-ghayb) — a divine-like attribute
Istighatha — seeking help from deceased saints in du'a (invoking the dead)
Mawlid celebrations as obligatory religious observance
Shrine visitation (urs) as a pious act
The Prophet ﷺ is present at gatherings where salawat is recited (hadir wa nazir)
scholarly_evaluation: Classified as deviant by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars due to specific theological positions. The core concerns are: (1) Istighatha — invoking deceased saints in du'a — is shirk per Athari aqeedah and the clear Quranic prohibition of calling upon other than Allah (al-Ahqaf 46:5). (2) Attributing knowledge of the unseen to the Prophet ﷺ contradicts explicit Quranic verses (al-A'raf 7:188, al-An'am 6:50). (3) The doctrine of hadir wa nazir (omnipresence of the Prophet ﷺ) contradicts Islamic monotheism. Individual Barelvi Muslims who love the Prophet ﷺ sincerely but do not knowingly commit shirk are not individually declared kafir — the doctrinal positions are what are evaluated.
sources: Kitab al-Tawhid (Ibn Abd al-Wahhab) — for refutation of istighatha
Al-Quran 46:5 — calling upon those who cannot hear

description_en: A grassroots Islamic missionary movement founded in Mewat, India in 1927 CE by Muhammad Ilyas Kandhlawi (1885–1944 CE). Emphasizes personal da'wah and revival through traveling in groups (khuruj) to mosques for three days, 40 days, or four months, calling Muslims back to prayer and basic Islamic practice. One of the largest Islamic movements in the world, with tens of millions of participants globally. Its 'Six Principles' (usul al-sitta) focus on kalima, salah, ilm wa dhikr, ikram al-Muslim, ikhlas al-niyyah, and dawah — largely avoiding political and controversial topics.
key_beliefs: Six Principles (usul al-sitta) as the methodology of revival
Khuruj (going out) for da'wah as a primary worship act
Avoidance of political, controversial, and scholarly debate topics
Derived from the Deobandi school in aqeedah and fiqh
scholarly_evaluation: Classified as deviant primarily due to three concerns: (1) Affiliation with the Deobandi school's issues. (2) Excessive restriction of da'wah topics — the movement avoids teaching aqeedah and condemning shirk, which means participants may remain exposed to deviant beliefs while improving their practice. Major Salafi scholars (Ibn Baz, al-Albani, Ibn Uthaymin) praised the sincerity of the movement but identified the gap in aqeedah education as a serious problem. (3) Some members develop cult-like attachment to the movement. The movement itself contains many sincere Muslims seeking to practice their deen — individual participants are not declared deviant for joining.
sources: Fada'il Amal (Muhammad Zakariyya Kandhlawi — primary Tablighi text, critiqued by scholars for weak and fabricated hadiths)
Cassette lectures of Ibn Baz on Tablighi Jamaat

description_en: A pan-Islamist political and social movement founded in Egypt in 1928 CE by Hasan al-Banna (1906–1949 CE). One of the most influential Islamist political organizations in the 20th century. Operates across dozens of countries. Emphasizes political participation to establish Islamic governance, social welfare work, and education. Its methodology and theological foundations have been extensively debated by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars.
key_beliefs: 'Islam is the solution' — Islamic governance as the primary goal
Political organization and participation as a religious duty
Gradual, democratic approach to establishing Islamic law
Social welfare, education, and charity as tools of influence
scholarly_evaluation: Ahl us-Sunnah scholars have raised several concerns: (1) Prioritization of political action over aqeedah rectification — al-Banna famously said 'We will not dispute in matters of fiqh' which led to coalition with groups of dubious aqeedah. (2) Hasan al-Banna's methodological principle of broad coalition means the organization has included Ash'ari, Sufi, and theologically deviant individuals without aqeedah correction. (3) The organization's alliance with the Muslim Brotherhood in some countries with groups that perform shirk (Sufi orders, etc.) is criticized. Scholars including al-Albani, Ibn Baz, and Ibn Uthaymin expressed concerns about the methodology while not necessarily declaring individual members deviant.
key_figures: Sayyid Qutb (1906–1966 CE) — later radical phase
sources: Al-Albani's recorded critiques of the Ikhwan methodology

description_en: A major Islamist political movement founded in British India in 1941 CE by Sayyid Abul Ala Mawdudi (1903–1979 CE). Currently active in Pakistan, Bangladesh, India, and Kashmir. Shares many methodological similarities with the Muslim Brotherhood and has similar scholarly critiques. Mawdudi's interpretations of Islamic governance and his comments on the Companions of the Prophet ﷺ have been specifically criticized by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars.
key_beliefs: Islamic state as the primary goal of the Islamic movement
Sovereignty (hakimiyyah) belongs to Allah — governments not ruling by Shari'ah are illegitimate
Gradual revolution through da'wah and political work
Comprehensive interpretation of Islam as a political system
scholarly_evaluation: Scholarly concerns include: (1) Mawdudi's statements critical of the Companions — including remarks about Uthman ibn Affan's governance — which were condemned by major scholars including Shaykh al-Albani and Mufti Shafi Usmani. (2) His concept of hakimiyyah (divine sovereignty) was critiqued by Ibn Baz and others for its political implications. (3) His tafsir (Tafhim al-Quran) contains positions criticized by classical scholars. His sincere contribution to Islamic scholarship is acknowledged, but specific positions are rejected.
sources: Mufti Shafi Usmani's response to Mawdudi on the Companions

description_en: A pan-Islamist political party founded in Jerusalem in 1953 CE by Taqiuddin al-Nabhani (1909–1977 CE). Aims to re-establish the Islamic Caliphate through political work, explicitly rejecting armed jihad as a current methodology. Active in dozens of countries. Banned in many Muslim-majority countries. Known for its distinctive ideological training program.
key_beliefs: Re-establishment of the Caliphate as the single obligatory political goal
Rejection of democracy as a system of governance
Rejection of nation-states and national borders as un-Islamic
Political da'wah and party work as the exclusive methodology — armed force explicitly rejected in current phase
scholarly_evaluation: Classified as deviant due to: (1) Its theological positions — al-Nabhani's writings contain Mu'tazilah-adjacent positions on divine attributes and human reason. (2) Prioritization of political methodology over aqeedah rectification — the same problem as the Muslim Brotherhood. (3) Declaring Muslim rulers kafir or agents of non-Muslims without sound evidence — a Kharijite-adjacent position. Banned as a terrorist organization in Russia, Germany, and multiple Muslim countries.

description_en: A militant ideology combining Salafi theological language with Qutbist political theory (from Sayyid Qutb's late works Milestones and In the Shade of the Quran) and Abdullah Azzam's globalized jihad methodology. Applied through organizations including al-Qaeda (founded 1988 CE) and later ISIS/Daesh (founded 2013 CE). Represents a Kharijite methodology in modern form — declaring Muslim rulers and ordinary Muslims kafir, considering their blood lawful, and using extreme violence against Muslim civilians.
key_beliefs: Takfir of Muslim rulers and governments not ruling by Shari'ah
Offensive armed jihad against Muslim rulers, their supporters, and 'apostate' states
Extreme version of hakimiyyah — sovereignty in governance as a pillar of tawhid
Killing of Muslim civilians permissible as 'collateral damage' in pursuit of Islamic state
scholarly_evaluation: Condemned unanimously by Ahl us-Sunnah scholars as a modern manifestation of the Kharijite methodology. The Prophet ﷺ described the Khawarij as 'the worst of creation' (Sahih Muslim 1066). The Council of Senior Scholars of Saudi Arabia, al-Azhar, and major Islamic institutions worldwide have issued unanimous condemnations. Scholars note the irony that the movement uses 'Salafi' language while violating the most fundamental Salafi principle — following the path of the Companions, who did not rebel against Muslim rulers.
key_figures: Abdullah Azzam (1941–1989 CE) — globalized jihad theory
sources: Council of Senior Scholars of Saudi Arabia — fatwa on terrorism
Al-Suyuf al-Batira (Ibn Baz — refutation of Kharijite methodology)

description_en: A broad movement that seeks to reform or reinterpret Islam in light of modern liberal values, including gender equality in all aspects including prayer leadership and mixed-gender prayer, full acceptance of homosexuality, and rejection of classical scholarly consensus on fiqh and aqeedah. Rejects the authority of classical Islamic scholarship in favor of personal reinterpretation. Not a formal organization but a loose intellectual current with significant presence in Western academic contexts.
key_beliefs: Reinterpretation of Quranic verses and hadiths to align with liberal values
Rejection of the scholarly consensus on gender roles in prayer and Islamic law
Acceptance of sexual ethics contrary to unanimous Islamic scholarly consensus
Islamic law (shari'ah) is an ongoing human construction, not divine command
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam as a doctrinal position. The specific positions — denial of the authority of the Quran's clear rulings, rejection of the Prophet's ﷺ explicit teachings, and declaration that Islamic scholarly consensus is a cultural construct to be overridden — constitute a denial of the fundamental authority of the Quran and Sunnah. This is not a difference of ijtihad (scholarly interpretation) but a rejection of the sources themselves. Note: Ordinary Muslims who may hold confused opinions on some issues are not individually declared kafir — the doctrinal platform as described is what places 'Progressive Islam' as a movement outside the fold.
key_figures: Amina Wadud (b. 1952 CE) — led mixed-gender prayer
Fazlur Rahman (1919–1988 CE) — Pakistani hermeneutic scholar

description_en: A religious movement founded in Qadian, Punjab (British India) in 1889 CE by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad (1835–1908 CE), who claimed to be the promised Messiah and Mahdi, and later claimed prophethood. The movement is divided into two factions: Qadiani (claims Mirza Ghulam Ahmad was a full prophet) and Lahori (claims he was a reformer, not a prophet). Ahmadis consider themselves Muslim but are officially declared non-Muslim by Islamic law and the Pakistani constitution.
key_beliefs: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad was a prophet after Muhammad ﷺ — the Promised Messiah and Mahdi
Jesus did not ascend to heaven but died in Kashmir
Rejection of physical jihad
British colonial rule supported as a legitimate authority for Muslims
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam by unanimous scholarly consensus across ALL Muslim groups — Sunni, Shia, and Sufi scholars alike. The Quran explicitly states Muhammad ﷺ is Khatam al-Nabiyyin (the Seal of the Prophets) (al-Ahzab 33:40). The Prophet ﷺ explicitly said there is no prophet after him (Sahih al-Bukhari 3455). Claiming prophethood after Muhammad ﷺ is an absolute violation of one of the most fundamental tenets of Islam, on which there is not even a minority scholarly opinion to the contrary. Declared non-Muslim by the Second Islamic Conference in Makkah (1974) and the Pakistani National Assembly (1974). In Pakistan the community is legally prohibited from calling themselves Muslim or performing Islamic practices.
sources: Resolution of the Second Islamic Conference, Makkah (1974)

description_en: An African-American political and religious organization founded in Detroit, Michigan in 1930 CE by Wallace Fard Muhammad, who claimed to be Allah incarnate. Its theology bears no relationship to Islamic monotheism: it teaches that white people are a race of devils created by a scientist named Yakub, that Wallace Fard is Allah, that Elijah Muhammad is a prophet, and that black people are the original divine race. Malcolm X and Muhammad Ali were both associated with the NOI before leaving it for Ahl us-Sunnah Islam.
key_beliefs: Wallace Fard Muhammad is Allah incarnate
Elijah Muhammad is a prophet of Allah
White people are genetically engineered 'blue-eyed devils'
Black people are the original divine race destined to rule
Racial separation as a religious imperative
scholarly_evaluation: Not Islam. The organization uses Islamic terminology but has no connection to Islamic monotheism, the Quran, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, or any recognized Muslim tradition. The claim that Wallace Fard is Allah, and that Elijah Muhammad is a prophet, violates both Tawhid (divine unity) and Khatam al-Nubuwwah (seal of prophethood) simultaneously. The racial theology has no basis in Islamic scripture whatsoever. Both Malcolm X (El-Hajj Malik el-Shabazz) and Imam Warith Deen Mohammed (Elijah Muhammad's son) left the NOI for mainstream Sunni Islam.
key_figures: Wallace Fard Muhammad (founded NOI, claimed to be Allah)
Elijah Muhammad (1897–1975 CE — NOI leader, claimed prophethood)
sources: Sahih al-Bukhari 3455 — no prophet after Muhammad ﷺ
Al-Quran 49:13 — the nobility is in taqwa, not race
Malcolm X autobiography — his departure from NOI after Hajj

description_en: A syncretic esoteric religion that emerged from Ismaili Shia Islam in 1017 CE, founded by Hamza ibn Ali and the Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (who declared himself divine). Incorporates elements of Islam, Neo-Platonism, Gnosticism, and pre-Islamic traditions. The Druze do not accept converts, do not follow the Five Pillars in the conventional sense (most do not pray, fast Ramadan, or perform Hajj), and consider their holy books secret. Concentrated in Lebanon, Syria, and Israel.
key_beliefs: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (Fatimid Caliph, d. 1021 CE) is the final manifestation of Allah
Reincarnation of souls (tanasukh) — denied by all Islamic scholarship
Secret esoteric texts accessible only to the initiated (uqqal)
The Five Pillars are allegorized — most Druze do not perform salah, Hajj, or fasting
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam by scholarly consensus. Declared kafir by Ibn Taymiyyah (Majmu' al-Fatawa 35:161), al-Qurtubi, Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, and all major Ahl us-Sunnah scholars. Their veneration of al-Hakim as divine, incorporation of reincarnationism, and abandonment of the Five Pillars constitute a departure from Islam that no minority scholarly opinion has ever defended. The Druze themselves do not claim to be Muslim in most contexts.
key_figures: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (985–1021 CE — venerated as divine

description_en: An esoteric religion founded by Muhammad ibn Nusayr (d. circa 868 CE), a disciple of the 10th Twelver Shia Imam al-Hadi. The Alawites venerate Ali ibn Abi Talib as a divine manifestation, incorporate elements of Christianity (celebration of Christmas and Epiphany), Neo-Platonism, and ancient Syrian religions. Their religious texts are kept secret from outsiders and even from lay Alawites. Concentrated in coastal Syria, where they form a significant minority and the Assad family is Alawi.
key_beliefs: Ali ibn Abi Talib is a divine manifestation — the highest expression of the divine
A divine triad: Muhammad, Ali, and Salman al-Farisi (as three aspects of the divine)
Celebration of Christian holidays (Christmas, Epiphany, Palm Sunday) and Nowruz
Secret religious texts accessible only to initiated males
Transmigration of souls (tanasukh)
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam by unanimous scholarly consensus. Declared kafir by Ibn Taymiyyah (in a famous fatwa requested by the Egyptian Mamluk Sultan), al-Qurtubi, and all classical Ahl us-Sunnah scholars who examined their beliefs. Their deification of Ali, incorporation of non-Islamic religious elements, and abandonment of the Islamic shari'ah place them categorically outside Islam. The Syrian Alawi community has historically presented itself as Muslim for political protection — a practice (taqiyyah) that itself has no precedent in Sunni Islam.
key_figures: Muhammad ibn Nusayr al-Namiri (d. circa 868 CE)
Al-Husayn ibn Hamdan al-Khasibi (d. 969 CE — major systematizer)
sources: Ibn Taymiyyah's fatwa on the Nusayriyyah (Majmu' al-Fatawa)

description_en: A religion that originated in Persia in 1844 CE when Siyyid Ali-Muhammad (the Bab) claimed to be a new prophet. His successor, Baha'u'llah (1817–1892 CE), claimed to be the prophet foretold by the Bab and the fulfillment of the prophecies of all major world religions. The Baha'i Faith is today an independent world religion with its own scriptures, laws, and global administrative structure headquartered in Haifa, Israel. It is explicitly NOT Islam.
key_beliefs: Progressive revelation — God sends successive prophets including the Bab and Baha'u'llah after Muhammad ﷺ
Unity of world religions — all major religions are valid paths
Equality of men and women in all matters
World government and universal peace as religious goals
scholarly_evaluation: Not Islam and never claimed to be a sect of Islam. The Baha'i Faith is a distinct world religion that originated by denying the finality of prophethood in Islam (Khatam al-Nubuwwah). It has its own scriptures, calendar, and laws. Declaring it a 'Muslim sect' would be inaccurate — it is an entirely separate religion that emerged from an Islamic context. Unanimous scholarly consensus across all Muslim groups.
sources: Baha'i World Faith (Baha'u'llah — their own scripture explicitly distinguishes from Islam)

description_en: A syncretic religious tradition originating in Anatolia (Turkey) in the medieval period, combining elements of Islam, Shia veneration of Ali, pre-Islamic Anatolian shamanism, and Central Asian Turkic traditions. Concentrated in Turkey (forming roughly 15–25% of the population). Alevis do not follow the Five Pillars conventionally: they do not typically perform the five daily prayers, do not observe Ramadan fasting, do not attend mosque, and do not perform Hajj. Their central worship form is the Cem ceremony, performed with music (saz) and communal gathering including men and women together.
key_beliefs: Veneration of Ali and the Twelve Imams (similar to Shia but more syncretic)
The Cem ceremony — communal worship with music, poetry, and dance — as the primary religious practice
The Five Pillars of Islam are allegorized or not performed conventionally
Absorption of pre-Islamic Anatolian shamanic and Turkic religious elements
scholarly_evaluation: Outside the fold of Islam per Ahl us-Sunnah scholarly consensus. While Alevis use Islamic terminology and identify culturally as Muslim, their religious practice has departed fundamentally from Islamic worship. The abandonment of the Five Pillars, the incorporation of music and mixed-gender worship not found in any Islamic tradition, and the syncretic absorption of non-Islamic elements place Alevism outside what can be defined as Islam. Many Alevis themselves distinguish their tradition from Sunni Islam and object to being classified under it.
key_figures: Haji Bektash Veli (1209–1271 CE) — major Alevi-Bektashi saint
Pir Sultan Abdal (16th century CE) — beloved Alevi poet
